caveats: a: The occupation mix covers newspapers, periodicals, books, and directories rather than book publishers alone.
a: Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not measure current automation.
a: Editorial and design occupations combine highly automatable preparation with human judgment and accountability.
rho: The AI-use measure is for the broad Information sector and any business function, not book publishing production deployment.
rho: Business adoption does not measure labor substitution or the depth of use.
rho: Copyrightability and training-rights uncertainty may constrain implementation beyond ordinary software adoption.
e: No direct audit of the missing LMM-band population or its service revenue mix was found.
e: Traditional author-publisher rights acquisition is not necessarily an outsourced service purchased by the author.
e: The frozen compensation input is an ancestor-sector proxy, and the dataset provides no defensible n value.
s5: The source is a qualitative trade-publishing deal review with no eligible-firm denominator.
s5: It includes international and large-publisher transactions outside the US LMM lens.
s5: Acquisitions of imprints or title lists may not transfer an operating firm.
q: No direct study measures post-AI benefit retention by book publishers.
q: AAP aggregates traditional publishing categories, formats, and channels rather than the eligible outsourced-service subset.
q: Savings may be competed into author terms, retail pricing, or additional title investment.
d5: AAP revenue is nominal and broader than the outsourced-service lens.
d5: One-year revenue growth and format shifts are not a five-year constant-quality volume forecast.
d5: Bestsellers and education purchasing can cause revenue changes unrelated to publishing workload.
o: No direct substitution study isolates operator requirement in NAICS 513130.
o: Operator need differs sharply across trade, education, professional, religious, university-press, and contract publishing.
o: Copyright rules require human authorship for protection but do not require a publishing firm.

envelope.interval_scope: research-input sensitivity with dataset anchors (l, n) held fixed

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Industry revenue is primarily title and course-material sales through physical and online retail, direct channels, and print, ebook, and audio formats, plus rights and licensing income. The eligible service subset can earn fixed production fees, royalties, revenue shares, or recurring program and list-management fees from authors, institutions, associations, and other content owners.
lens.excluded_activities: Newspaper and periodical publishers, software publishers, printers without publishing activity, retailers and distributors that do not publish, author-operated single-title vehicles, captive internal presses, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and publishers that only acquire content rights and sell their own books without supplying a recurring or repeat outsourced service to an external customer.
lens.included_activities: United States lower-middle-market book publishers whose operations include repeat design, editing, production coordination, rights administration, marketing, and distribution of print, electronic, or audio books, with eligibility assessed for recurring or repeat outsourced publishing relationships with external authors, institutions, associations, or content owners.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 513100 publishing group rather than six-digit book publishers and reports management at 12.78 percent of employment, business and financial operations at 10.68 percent, computer and mathematical occupations at 8.71 percent, arts and media at 29.00 percent, sales at 13.37 percent, and office and administrative support at 16.13 percent; editors alone are 13.13 percent. BLS duty descriptions map editing, proofreading, fact checking, manuscript screening, layout, metadata, marketing analysis, customer service, and routine administration to AI-exposed tasks. Book publishers likely have more editorial and rights work than the parent group, raising exposure, but acquisition judgment, author development, art direction, negotiation, and final accountability lower substitution relative to raw information-work shares.
caveats: The occupation mix covers newspapers, periodicals, books, and directories rather than book publishers alone.
Employment shares are not wage-weighted task shares and do not measure current automation.
Editorial and design occupations combine highly automatable preparation with human judgment and accountability.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit wage-weighted time study from book publishers separating acquisitions, developmental and copy editing, design, production, rights, royalties, marketing, sales, and fulfillment tasks, net of automation already in use, would materially change the range.
a.rationale: Manuscript triage, copyediting assistance, fact-check preparation, metadata, jacket and catalog copy, translation drafts, accessibility conversion, layout preparation, marketing variants, sales analysis, rights records, royalty support, and customer service are information-intensive. Editorial taste, author relationships, commissioning, legal review, pedagogy, brand decisions, and final approval remain human-led.

d5.bridge: AAP estimates US publishing revenue across books and course materials rose 4.1 percent in 2024 to $32.5 billion, reports print above half of industry revenue for each of five years, and reports digital-audio revenue nearly 80 percent above 2020. These nominal revenue and format signals are translated into modest five-year constant-price, constant-quality demand growth for book-publishing services. Inflation, best-seller cycles, education budgets, format mix, direct self-publishing, and retailer power can move revenue independently of service quantity in either direction.
caveats: AAP revenue is nominal and broader than the outsourced-service lens.
One-year revenue growth and format shifts are not a five-year constant-quality volume forecast.
Bestsellers and education purchasing can cause revenue changes unrelated to publishing workload.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year forecast of inflation-adjusted US book-publisher net revenue and units by category and format, new-title counts, course adoption, library purchasing, self-publishing substitution, and eligible-service contract renewals would materially change the multiplier.
d5.rationale: Persistent reading, education, professional information, backlist monetization, and growth in audio and online channels support continued publishing demand. Population shifts, school procurement, discretionary spending, attention competition, open educational resources, self-publishing, and AI-generated content create a broad range that includes decline.

caveats: No direct audit of the missing LMM-band population or its service revenue mix was found.
Traditional author-publisher rights acquisition is not necessarily an outsourced service purchased by the author.
The frozen compensation input is an ancestor-sector proxy, and the dataset provides no defensible n value.
e.change_evidence: A six-digit firm census combining publisher websites and contracts with customer type, revenue by title sales versus external service fees, repeat-client share, normalized EBITDA, captive status, and transferable rights would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: The official industry definition centers on publishers designing, editing, marketing, producing, and distributing books, usually as owners or licensees of title economics rather than outsourced service providers. A minority of LMM firms plausibly provide repeat contract publishing, institutional programs, association publishing, sponsored series, or author-service relationships that fit the lens.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 51 ($506,769,522,547) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 51 ($2,139,449,301,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: ancestor 51. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.3593 (margins.json[513]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Software (System & Application) EBITDA/Sales 35.93% (NAICS 2022 513 = publishing incl. software)); revenue/employee $588,729 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 51); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: No direct substitution study isolates operator requirement in NAICS 513130.
Operator need differs sharply across trade, education, professional, religious, university-press, and contract publishing.
Copyright rules require human authorship for protection but do not require a publishing firm.
o.change_evidence: Cohort evidence comparing operator-led, platform-assisted, and AI-led publishing on net sales, author economics, completion and error rates, rights disputes, retailer access, discoverability, adoption, and total cost would materially change the operator-required share.
o.rationale: Authors and institutions can increasingly self-publish, use platform tools, or automate editing, layout, metadata, translation, and promotion. An accountable publisher remains valuable for rights acquisition, developmental editing, author management, brand and imprint trust, permissions, pedagogy, fact and legal review, distribution access, marketing coordination, and final publication decisions. Copyright protection for AI-assisted work still turns on sufficient human-authored expressive elements, reinforcing human oversight without requiring every current publisher function.

caveats: No direct study measures post-AI benefit retention by book publishers.
AAP aggregates traditional publishing categories, formats, and channels rather than the eligible outsourced-service subset.
Savings may be competed into author terms, retail pricing, or additional title investment.
q.change_evidence: Publisher-level cohorts linking contract type, author royalties, retailer discounts, service pricing, AI and assurance cost, labor hours per title, title throughput, renewal pricing, and operating contribution would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Fixed-price books, fixed service fees, advance-backed publishing programs, and owned or licensed backlists can let operators retain implemented efficiency until the next contract or title cycle. Retention is reduced by author royalties and revenue shares, retailer and distributor bargaining, competitive advances, institution procurement, lower self-publishing costs, AI vendor expense, and pressure to reinvest in title volume and marketing.

rho.bridge: Census BTOS measures business-level AI use across the two-digit Information sector, not the fraction of exposed book-publishing task-hours implemented. As of May 3, 2026, 39.7 percent of Information businesses reported current AI use and about 42 percent expected use within six months. Those adoption levels are translated into five-year workflow implementation, with structured digital manuscripts and metadata pushing upward while copyright, author consent, hallucination, brand, workflow integration, and quality-control requirements push realized substitution below broad technical exposure.
caveats: The AI-use measure is for the broad Information sector and any business function, not book publishing production deployment.
Business adoption does not measure labor substitution or the depth of use.
Copyrightability and training-rights uncertainty may constrain implementation beyond ordinary software adoption.
rho.change_evidence: A matched five-year panel of book publishers reporting AI workflow coverage, editor and production hours per title, error and recall rates, author consent, rights disputes, time to publication, vendor cost, and avoided hiring would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Publishers already operate digital manuscript, production, rights, marketing, and commerce systems that can embed AI assistance. Implementation will be uneven because backlists, permissions, house style, author contracts, training-data rights, confidential submissions, factual accuracy, and reputational risk require governance and human review.

s5.bridge: Publishers Weekly describes active 2024 trade-publishing M&A, including several Big Five purchases, no Big Five target, and an outlook of many niche purchases rather than blockbuster deals. This observed but non-enumerated trade-publishing deal flow is translated into a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible US LMM service-oriented book publishers. Valuable backlists, rights, imprints, and distribution relationships support transfers, while author dependence, disputed rights, small scale, mission ownership, and the absence of a transaction denominator may make the target probability lower than the visible deal narrative suggests.
caveats: The source is a qualitative trade-publishing deal review with no eligible-firm denominator.
It includes international and large-publisher transactions outside the US LMM lens.
Acquisitions of imprints or title lists may not transfer an operating firm.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year US transaction census from Publishers Marketplace, PitchBook, company filings, and publisher announcements, matched to eligible LMM firms and including failed processes, asset deals, earnouts, and post-sale author retention, would materially change the probability.
s5.rationale: Strategic buyers can acquire specialist lists, backlists, editorial brands, distribution relationships, and institutional contracts. Transferability falls when value resides in a founder's taste or author relationships, contracts are nonassignable, rights are fragmented, or the operation is a university, nonprofit, or single-title vehicle.

principal_driver: Repeatable editorial preparation, production, metadata, marketing, rights administration, and service operations create substantial implementable AI work.
principal_weakness: Most industry firms monetize books and rights as products rather than recurring outsourced services, while the eligible LMM firm count is missing.
